title_en: How little exercise can you get away with?
rubric_en: Even tiny bursts of activity have measurable benefits
rubric_zh: 即使是短暂的活动也带来可测量的好处

paragraphs.en: FEW THINGS are as unambiguously good for you as exercise. Besides making you fitter and stronger, it cuts your risk of heart disease, strokes, diabetes, many types of cancer and more.
paragraphs.zh: 没有多少东西能像运动那样明确地对你有益。除了让你更健康、更强壮之外，它还能降低你罹患心脏病、中风、糖尿病、许多类型癌症等疾病的风险。

paragraphs.en: But how much of the cure-all do you really need? The World Health Organisation (WHO) recommends at least 150 minutes a week of moderate activity, such as easy jogging or cycling, and two sessions a week of strength training. Many still fall short. In America, for instance, less than half of adults meet the minimum recommendations for just cardiovascular exercise alone.
paragraphs.zh: 但到底需要多少“万能药”才够呢？世界卫生组织（WHO）建议每周至少进行150分钟中等强度活动，例如轻松慢跑或骑自行车，并每周进行两次力量训练。许多人仍未达标。例如，在美国，仅有不到一半的成年人在有氧运动方面就达到最低建议量。

paragraphs.en: Those who hate pounding the pavements need not despair, though. Even a little exercise is better than none. And a growing pile of evidence suggests that might be true even with very small amounts of exercise indeed: potentially just a few minutes a day.
paragraphs.zh: 那些不喜欢在路上快走或乱跑的人无需绝望。任何一点运动都比没有好。而且越来越多的证据表明，即使运动量非常少也可能有效：甚至可能只要每天几分钟。

paragraphs.en: Scientists have long known that the returns to exercise are not constant. The biggest benefits come from doing any at all. Doing lots is better than doing just a little, but the relative improvement is smaller. A meta-analysis published in 2015 pooled data covering around 660,000 people in America and compared the amount of exercise participants claimed to do with the number who died over the following years (the average follow-up period was 14 years).
paragraphs.zh: 科学家们早已知道，运动的收益并不是线性增长的。最大收益来自于从“零”开始做起。多运动总比少运动好，但边际收益会变小。2015年发表的一项荟萃分析汇总了约66万人在美国的数据，将受试者自报的运动量与其后若干年内的死亡人数进行了比较（平均随访期为14年）。

paragraphs.en: Compared with those who did no exercise at all, people who reported doing less than the WHO’s minimum had a 20% lower chance of dying over the follow-up period. Those who did between one and two times as much as the WHO guidelines saw their risk fall by 31%. The truly keen, who did two to three times more than advised, saw a drop of 37%—a much smaller jump than those who went from nothing to just a little.
paragraphs.zh: 与完全不运动的人相比，那些报告每周运动量低于世界卫生组织最低建议的人，随访期间死亡风险低了20%。而每周运动量达到世界卫生组织建议的1到2倍的人，死亡风险下降了31%。真正积极的人群——每天运动量为建议值的2到3倍——则下降了37%，这比“从完全不运动到稍微运动一点”带来的改善幅度要小得多。

paragraphs.en: One problem with such studies is that they rely on self-reporting, which is often unreliable. Newer studies use wearable sensors, which are more reliable and which allow more granular measurements. They suggest that even tiny, irregular spurts of exercise can have powerful health benefits.
paragraphs.zh: 这类研究的一个问题是往往依赖自我报告，而自我报告通常不够可靠。较新的研究使用可穿戴传感器，数据更可靠，也能做更细致的测量。它们表明，即使是非常短小且不规律的运动爆发，也能带来显著的健康益处。

paragraphs.en: In 2022 a group of researchers published a study that crunched data from 25,000 British people who did no organised exercise. The researchers were interested in “vigorous intermittent lifestyle physical activity”, or VILPA—short bursts of relatively intense exercise undertaken in daily life, such as running for a bus or climbing steep stairs.
paragraphs.zh: 2022年，一组研究人员发布了一项研究，分析了25000名不进行有组织运动的英国人的数据。研究者关注的是“高强度间歇性生活体力活动”，即VILPA（vigorous intermittent lifestyle physical activity），指的是日常生活中进行的短时间高强度运动，例如为了赶公交而快速奔跑或快速爬陡楼梯。

paragraphs.en: Few of the participants did much VILPA—the median was just 4.4 minutes per day. But even that was associated with a reduction in the chance of dying, from any cause, of up to 30% in the following seven years for which the average participant was monitored. The correlation persisted even after the researchers tried to account for the idea that causality might run the other way, with only the already healthy capable of performing VILPA.
paragraphs.zh: 很少有参与者做了很多VILPA——中位数每天仅为4.4分钟。但即便如此，这也与其后7年随访期内全因死亡率下降有关，降幅高达30%。即使研究者试图排除“因果关系可能反过来”的解释（即只有身体本来就健康的人才有能力进行VILPA），这种相关性仍然成立。

paragraphs.en: Exactly why VILPAs seem to be so good for you is not yet clear (one theory is that they work in essentially the same way as high-intensity interval training, a quick but demanding method of getting into shape). But inspired by the results, some academics have started advocating “exercise snacks”: short bursts of activity that even the most gym-shy or time-pressed should be able to manage. Do some bodyweight squats while waiting for the kettle to boil, say, or knock out some star jumps during a dull Zoom meeting (but don’t forget to turn the camera off first). As the old saying goes, every little helps. In fact, it may help much more than you think. ■
paragraphs.zh: 为什么 VILPA（高强度间歇性日常运动）看起来对健康如此有益，目前还不完全清楚（有一种理论认为，它的作用机理与高强度间歇训练相似，即一种快速但强度较高的健身方式）。但受这些结果启发，一些学者开始提倡“运动零食”：即使是最不爱去健身房、最忙不过来的人，也应该能够完成的短暂活动。比如在等水壶烧开时做几下徒手深蹲，或是在一场无聊的 Zoom 会议里做几组开合跳（但别忘了先把摄像头关掉）。俗话说，哪怕很少一点也有帮助。实际上，它可能比你想象的帮助更大。■

paragraphs.en: After a free, evidence-based guide to health and wellness? Sign up to our weekly Well Informed newsletter.
paragraphs.zh: 想要获取一份免费的、基于证据的健康与养生指南吗？请订阅我们的《Well Informed》每周通讯。
